[[Category:kk]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:dd]] [[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kyōsai Kawanabe|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered {{ill|Tabunoki|ja|タブノキ}} seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for {{ill|Mount Bandai|ja|磐梯山}}. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amatenagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|San Cai Tu Hui|ja|三才図会}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for {{ill|Mount Bandai|ja|磐梯山}}. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amatenagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|San Cai Tu Hui|ja|三才図会}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kyōsai Kawanabe|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered {{ill|Tabunoki|ja|タブノキ}} seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for {{ill|Mount Bandai|ja|磐梯山}}. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amatenagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|San Cai Tu Hui|ja|三才図会}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kyōsai Kawanabe|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered {{ill|Tabunoki|ja|タブノキ}} seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for {{ill|Mount Bandai|ja|磐梯山}}. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amatenagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|San Cai Tu Hui|ja|三才図会}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kyōsai Kawanabe|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered {{ill|Tabunoki|ja|タブノキ}} seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for {{ill|Mount Bandai|ja|磐梯山}}. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kyōsai Kawanabe|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

[[Category:kk]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kyōsai Kawanabe|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Giants (mythology)]]

[[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kyōsai Kawanabe|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Giants (mythology)]]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kyōsai Kawanabe|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Giants (mythology)]]

The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kyōsai Kawanabe|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Giants (mythology)]]

In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

[[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] {{Omonoimi Faith}}[[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Giants (mythology)]]

They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the ]][[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged ]][[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=]][[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref> Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered ]][[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />=== Fukushima ===In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for ]][[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>=== Fukui ===At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings. According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>== Influence from China ==Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as ]][[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>== Cultural Depictions ==The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" /><gallery>File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa
