{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as ]][[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|]][[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}}]][[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]]'''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as ]][[Akita Prefecture]], ]][[Yamagata Prefecture]], ]][[Fukushima Prefecture]], ]][[Nagano Prefecture]], and ]][[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=]][[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>== Overview ==Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.== Local Legends and Folktales ===== Akita and Yamagata ===In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on ]][[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the ]][[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged ]][[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=]][[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref> Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered ]][[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />=== Fukushima ===In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for ]][[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>=== Fukui ===At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings. According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>== Influence from China ==Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as ]][[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. {{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

[[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] {{Omonoimi Faith}}[[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

[[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] {{Omonoimi Faith}}[[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=draft}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=draft}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従|12=draft}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市|12=draft}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=draft}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭|12=draft}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨|12=draft}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳|12=draft}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

{{draft categories| [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:Tenaga and Ashinaga]] [[Category:Giants]] [[Category:Japanese folklore]] [[Category:Omonoimi Faith]] [[Category:Chokaisan Faith]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Suwa Faith topics in the template that need translation to English]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:手長足長]] [[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎|12=draft}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=draft}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁|12=draft}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社|12=draft}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=draft}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=draft}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|12=draft}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>

Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.

In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市|12=draft}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=draft}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>

Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭|12=draft}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨|12=draft}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳|12=draft}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=draft}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=draft}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|12=draft}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=draft}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=draft}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=draft}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従|12=draft}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市|12=draft}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=draft}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭|12=draft}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨|12=draft}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳|12=draft}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎|12=draft}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=draft}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁|12=draft}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社|12=draft}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=draft}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=draft}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|12=draft}}. Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭|12=draft}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨|12=draft}}.<ref name="murakami" />

They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=draft|12=draft}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁|12=draft|12=draft}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社|12=draft|12=draft}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=draft|12=draft}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=draft|12=draft}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|12=draft|12=draft}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=draft|12=draft}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=draft|12=draft}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従|12=draft|12=draft}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=draft|12=draft}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳|12=draft|12=draft}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

[[ja:手長足長]] [[en:Tenaga-Ashikaga]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

revision.comment: 75 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:手長足長 up to rev 100247580
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|日本各地に伝わる[[巨人 (伝説の生物)|巨人]]の手長足長|[[妖怪]]の足長手長|足長手長}} [[画像:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|『手長足長図』[[河鍋暁斎]]・画]] '''手長足長'''（てながあしなが）は、[[秋田県]]、[[山形県]]、[[福島県]]、[[長野県]]、[[福井県]]などに伝わる伝説・昔話に登場する[[巨人 (伝説の生物)|巨人]]<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|和書|author=村上健司編著|authorlink=村上健司|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=[[毎日新聞社]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>。
== 概要 == その特徴は｢手足が異常に長い巨人｣で各地の伝説は共通しているが、手足の長い一人の巨人、または夫の足（脚）が異常に長く妻の手（腕）が異様に長い夫婦、または兄弟の巨人とも言われ、各地で細部は異なることもある。手の長いほうが「手長」足が長いほうが「足長」として表現される。
== 各地の社伝、昔話 == === 秋田・山形 === 秋田では[[鳥海山]]に棲んでいたとされ、山から山に届くほど長い手足を持ち、旅人をさらって食べたり、[[日本海]]を行く船を襲うなどの悪事を働いていた。鳥海山の神である[[大物忌神]]はこれを見かね、霊鳥である三本足の[[カラス|鴉]]を遣わせ、手長足長が現れるときには「有や」現れないときには「無や」と鳴かせて人々に知らせるようにした。山のふもとの[[三崎公園 (秋田県・山形県)|三崎峠]]が「有耶無耶の関」と呼ばれるのはこれが由来とされる<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=三崎公園・有耶無耶の関|work=[http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/ 湧水の里 遊佐町]|publisher=[[遊佐町]]産業振興課|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>。それでも手長足長の悪行は続いたため、後にこの地を訪れた[[円仁|慈覚大師]]が吹浦（山形県[[鳥海山大物忌神社]]）で百日間祈りを捧げた末、鳥海山は吹き飛んで手長足長が消え去ったという<ref name="murakami" />。また消えたのではなく、大師の前に降参して人を食べなくなったともいわれ、大師がこの地を去るときに手長足長のために食糧として[[タブノキ]]の実を撒いたことから、現在でも三崎山（[[にかほ市]]小砂川地域）にはタブノキが茂っているのだという<ref name="yuza" />。
=== 福島 === 福島の[[会津若松市|会津若松]]に出現したとされる手長足長は、病悩山（びょうのうざん、やもうさん、わずらわしやま。[[磐梯山]]の古名）の頂上に住み着き、会津の空を雲で被い、その地で作物ができない状態にする非道を行い、この状態を長期にわたり続けたという。その地を偶然訪れた旅の僧侶がことの事情を知り、病悩山山頂へ赴き、手長足長を病悩山の頂上に封印し、磐梯明神として祀ったとされている<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html ふるさと福島紀行 第三十六回 磐梯山と弘法大師] - 福島県南酒販株式会社</ref>。このことをきっかけに、病悩山は[[磐梯山]]と改められ、手長足長を封印した旅の僧侶こそ、各地を修行中の[[空海|弘法大師（空海）]]だったと言われている。
=== 福井 === 福井の[[雄島]]にある大湊神社には、[[東尋坊|安島]]に最初に住んでいたのが手長と足長だったと伝わる。足長が手長を背負って海に入り、手長が貝のフンをその長い手で海に入れ、魚をおびき寄せ獲って暮らしていたという<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=安島の住人「手長足長」|work=[http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/index2.html Web大湊神社]|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>。
== 神仙としての手長・足長 == 上記のような荒ぶる巨人としての存在とは別に、神・巨人・眷属神としての手長足長、不老長寿の神仙としての手長足長もみられる。
[[室町時代]]に編纂された『[[大日本国一宮記]]』によると、[[壱岐]]（[[長崎県]]）では[[天手長男神社]]が国の[[一宮|一の宮]]であった<ref>『大日本国一宮記』（『[[群書類従]]』第2輯 神祇部 1932年） 246頁</ref> とされ、天手長男（あめのたながお）神社と天手長比売（あめのたながひめ）神社の2社が存在していた。
長野の[[上諏訪町]]（現・[[諏訪市]]）では、手長足長は[[諏訪大社|諏訪明神]]の家来とされており<ref name="murakami" />、手長と足長の夫婦の神であるといわれ、手長足長を祀る[[手長神社]]、[[足長神社]]が存在する<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=いちごの神社参拝物語 手長神社 足長神社|work=[https://web.archive.org/web/20040703161229/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/index.html 心霊と神霊・自然と神霊]|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>。この二社は記紀神話に登場している出雲の神である[[奇稲田姫]]（くしなだひめ）の父母・[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|足名稚（あしなづち）と手名稚（てなづち）]]が祭神とされているが、それは巨人ではないかという説<ref name="yanagita">{{Cite book|和書|author=柳田國男|authorlink=柳田國男|title=一目小僧その他|year=2013|publisher=[[角川書店]]|isbn=978-4-04-408308-3|pages=291-292}}</ref>、ユダヤ系人物ではないかという説<ref>[[田中英道]]</ref>がある。また、[[建御名方神]]（たけみなかたのかみ）が諏訪に侵入する以前に、諏訪を支配していた神の一つで、[[洩矢神]]（もりやのかみ）と共に建御名方神と戦ったとされる。
これら社寺に関連する「てなが（手長）」という言葉について[[柳田國男]]は、中世ころまで[[給仕]]者や従者を意味していた「てなが」という言葉が先にあり、「手の長い」巨人のような存在となったのは後の時代でのことであろうと推測している<ref name="yanagita" />。
=== 中国からの伝播 === 『[[大鏡]]』（[[11世紀]]末成立）第3巻「伊尹伝」（これただ/これまさ・でん）には、硯箱（すずりばこ）に[[蓬萊山]]・手長・足長などを金[[蒔絵]]にして作らせたということが記されており、[[花山天皇|花山院]]（[[10世紀]]末）の頃には、空想上の人物たる手長・足長が認知されていたことがわかる。これは王圻『[[三才図会]]』などに収録されている中国に伝わる[[長臂人]]（ちょうひじん）・[[長股人]]（ちょうこじん）（[[足長手長]]）を神仙図のひとつとして描くことによって天皇の長寿を願ったと考えられる<ref>[[保坂弘司]]著 『大鏡 全現代語訳』 [[講談社学術文庫]] 第23刷1998年（初版1981年）、 287頁-289頁。 ISBN 4-06-158491-X。</ref>。天皇の御所である[[清涼殿]]にある「荒磯障子」に同画題は描かれており、[[清少納言]]の『[[枕草子]]』<ref>『枕草子』 20段 「清涼殿のうしとらのすみの北のへだてなる御障子は荒海のかたいきたる物どもおそろしげなる手長足長などをぞかきたる。上の御つぼねの戸をおしあけたれば常に目に見ゆるをにくみなどしてわらふ。」</ref> にもこの障子の絵についての記述が見られる。
== 文化 == [[物語|物語文学]]のひとつである[[絵巻物]]『宇治橋姫物語絵巻』には、主人公のひとりである中将を取り囲んで現われる異形の存在（「色々の姿したる人々」）として、みるめ・かぐはな・手なが・あしながという名が文章上では挙げられている<ref>[[楢崎宗重]]「住吉如慶筆 宇治橋姫物語絵巻」『國華』907 國華社 1967年 19頁</ref>。
[[岐阜県]][[高山市]]の[[高山祭|飛騨高山祭]]の[[山車]]（だし）装飾、市内の橋の欄干の彫刻など手長足長のモチーフが多く見られる<ref name="murakami" /><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.hidanavi.com/te-ashi.html|title=手長像、足長像とは|work=[http://www.hidanavi.com/ 飛騨高山観光ナビ]|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>。これは[[嘉永]]年間の宮大工が彫刻を手名稚と足名稚として高山祭屋台に取り付けたものが由来<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://gdn2425.jp/statue/s_takayama/statue_02/|title=がんでん橋|accessdate=2016-05-31}}</ref> とされている。手長足長に神仙としてのイメージと、『[[山海経]]』や『[[異国物語]]』あるいは[[浮世絵]]などの絵画作品を通じての異民族・妖怪としてのイメージ、双方からのイメージが江戸時代後期には出来上がっていることがわかる。 <gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|「浅草奥山[[生人形]]」[[歌川国芳]] 安政2年 File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|[[根付け]]Hideaki (or Shūmei) 19th File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|根付け Matsushige 18th File:Unryôsai Hiroyasu - Tsuba with Legendary Figures by the Seashore - Walters 51147.jpg|[[鍔]] Unryôsai Hiroyasu 19th </gallery>
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Ashinagatenaga}} * [[巨人]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[手長足長]] to [[Tenaga and Ashinaga]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|日本各地に伝わる[[巨人 (伝説の生物)|巨人]]の手長足長|[[妖怪]]の足長手長|足長手長}} [[画像:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|『手長足長図』[[河鍋暁斎]]・画]] '''手長足長'''（てながあしなが）は、[[秋田県]]、[[山形県]]、[[福島県]]、[[長野県]]、[[福井県]]などに伝わる伝説・昔話に登場する[[巨人 (伝説の生物)|巨人]]<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|和書|author=村上健司編著|authorlink=村上健司|title=妖怪事典|year=2000|publisher=[[毎日新聞社]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>。
== 概要 == その特徴は｢手足が異常に長い巨人｣で各地の伝説は共通しているが、手足の長い一人の巨人、または夫の足（脚）が異常に長く妻の手（腕）が異様に長い夫婦、または兄弟の巨人とも言われ、各地で細部は異なることもある。手の長いほうが「手長」足が長いほうが「足長」として表現される。
== 各地の社伝、昔話 == === 秋田・山形 === 秋田では[[鳥海山]]に棲んでいたとされ、山から山に届くほど長い手足を持ち、旅人をさらって食べたり、[[日本海]]を行く船を襲うなどの悪事を働いていた。鳥海山の神である[[大物忌神]]はこれを見かね、霊鳥である三本足の[[カラス|鴉]]を遣わせ、手長足長が現れるときには「有や」現れないときには「無や」と鳴かせて人々に知らせるようにした。山のふもとの[[三崎公園 (秋田県・山形県)|三崎峠]]が「有耶無耶の関」と呼ばれるのはこれが由来とされる<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=三崎公園・有耶無耶の関|work=[http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/ 湧水の里 遊佐町]|publisher=[[遊佐町]]産業振興課|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>。それでも手長足長の悪行は続いたため、後にこの地を訪れた[[円仁|慈覚大師]]が吹浦（山形県[[鳥海山大物忌神社]]）で百日間祈りを捧げた末、鳥海山は吹き飛んで手長足長が消え去ったという<ref name="murakami" />。また消えたのではなく、大師の前に降参して人を食べなくなったともいわれ、大師がこの地を去るときに手長足長のために食糧として[[タブノキ]]の実を撒いたことから、現在でも三崎山（[[にかほ市]]小砂川地域）にはタブノキが茂っているのだという<ref name="yuza" />。
=== 福島 === 福島の[[会津若松市|会津若松]]に出現したとされる手長足長は、病悩山（びょうのうざん、やもうさん、わずらわしやま。[[磐梯山]]の古名）の頂上に住み着き、会津の空を雲で被い、その地で作物ができない状態にする非道を行い、この状態を長期にわたり続けたという。その地を偶然訪れた旅の僧侶がことの事情を知り、病悩山山頂へ赴き、手長足長を病悩山の頂上に封印し、磐梯明神として祀ったとされている<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html ふるさと福島紀行 第三十六回 磐梯山と弘法大師] - 福島県南酒販株式会社</ref>。このことをきっかけに、病悩山は[[磐梯山]]と改められ、手長足長を封印した旅の僧侶こそ、各地を修行中の[[空海|弘法大師（空海）]]だったと言われている。
=== 福井 === 福井の[[雄島]]にある大湊神社には、[[東尋坊|安島]]に最初に住んでいたのが手長と足長だったと伝わる。足長が手長を背負って海に入り、手長が貝のフンをその長い手で海に入れ、魚をおびき寄せ獲って暮らしていたという<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=安島の住人「手長足長」|work=[http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/index2.html Web大湊神社]|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>。
== 神仙としての手長・足長 == 上記のような荒ぶる巨人としての存在とは別に、神・巨人・眷属神としての手長足長、不老長寿の神仙としての手長足長もみられる。
[[室町時代]]に編纂された『[[大日本国一宮記]]』によると、[[壱岐]]（[[長崎県]]）では[[天手長男神社]]が国の[[一宮|一の宮]]であった<ref>『大日本国一宮記』（『[[群書類従]]』第2輯 神祇部 1932年） 246頁</ref> とされ、天手長男（あめのたながお）神社と天手長比売（あめのたながひめ）神社の2社が存在していた。
長野の[[上諏訪町]]（現・[[諏訪市]]）では、手長足長は[[諏訪大社|諏訪明神]]の家来とされており<ref name="murakami" />、手長と足長の夫婦の神であるといわれ、手長足長を祀る[[手長神社]]、[[足長神社]]が存在する<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=いちごの神社参拝物語 手長神社 足長神社|work=[https://web.archive.org/web/20040703161229/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/index.html 心霊と神霊・自然と神霊]|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>。この二社は記紀神話に登場している出雲の神である[[奇稲田姫]]（くしなだひめ）の父母・[[アシナヅチ・テナヅチ|足名稚（あしなづち）と手名稚（てなづち）]]が祭神とされているが、それは巨人ではないかという説<ref name="yanagita">{{Cite book|和書|author=柳田國男|authorlink=柳田國男|title=一目小僧その他|year=2013|publisher=[[角川書店]]|isbn=978-4-04-408308-3|pages=291-292}}</ref>、ユダヤ系人物ではないかという説<ref>[[田中英道]]</ref>がある。また、[[建御名方神]]（たけみなかたのかみ）が諏訪に侵入する以前に、諏訪を支配していた神の一つで、[[洩矢神]]（もりやのかみ）と共に建御名方神と戦ったとされる。
これら社寺に関連する「てなが（手長）」という言葉について[[柳田國男]]は、中世ころまで[[給仕]]者や従者を意味していた「てなが」という言葉が先にあり、「手の長い」巨人のような存在となったのは後の時代でのことであろうと推測している<ref name="yanagita" />。
=== 中国からの伝播 === 『[[大鏡]]』（[[11世紀]]末成立）第3巻「伊尹伝」（これただ/これまさ・でん）には、硯箱（すずりばこ）に[[蓬萊山]]・手長・足長などを金[[蒔絵]]にして作らせたということが記されており、[[花山天皇|花山院]]（[[10世紀]]末）の頃には、空想上の人物たる手長・足長が認知されていたことがわかる。これは王圻『[[三才図会]]』などに収録されている中国に伝わる[[長臂人]]（ちょうひじん）・[[長股人]]（ちょうこじん）（[[足長手長]]）を神仙図のひとつとして描くことによって天皇の長寿を願ったと考えられる<ref>[[保坂弘司]]著 『大鏡 全現代語訳』 [[講談社学術文庫]] 第23刷1998年（初版1981年）、 287頁-289頁。 ISBN 4-06-158491-X。</ref>。天皇の御所である[[清涼殿]]にある「荒磯障子」に同画題は描かれており、[[清少納言]]の『[[枕草子]]』<ref>『枕草子』 20段 「清涼殿のうしとらのすみの北のへだてなる御障子は荒海のかたいきたる物どもおそろしげなる手長足長などをぞかきたる。上の御つぼねの戸をおしあけたれば常に目に見ゆるをにくみなどしてわらふ。」</ref> にもこの障子の絵についての記述が見られる。
== 文化 == [[物語|物語文学]]のひとつである[[絵巻物]]『宇治橋姫物語絵巻』には、主人公のひとりである中将を取り囲んで現われる異形の存在（「色々の姿したる人々」）として、みるめ・かぐはな・手なが・あしながという名が文章上では挙げられている<ref>[[楢崎宗重]]「住吉如慶筆 宇治橋姫物語絵巻」『國華』907 國華社 1967年 19頁</ref>。
[[岐阜県]][[高山市]]の[[高山祭|飛騨高山祭]]の[[山車]]（だし）装飾、市内の橋の欄干の彫刻など手長足長のモチーフが多く見られる<ref name="murakami" /><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.hidanavi.com/te-ashi.html|title=手長像、足長像とは|work=[http://www.hidanavi.com/ 飛騨高山観光ナビ]|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>。これは[[嘉永]]年間の宮大工が彫刻を手名稚と足名稚として高山祭屋台に取り付けたものが由来<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://gdn2425.jp/statue/s_takayama/statue_02/|title=がんでん橋|accessdate=2016-05-31}}</ref> とされている。手長足長に神仙としてのイメージと、『[[山海経]]』や『[[異国物語]]』あるいは[[浮世絵]]などの絵画作品を通じての異民族・妖怪としてのイメージ、双方からのイメージが江戸時代後期には出来上がっていることがわかる。 <gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|「浅草奥山[[生人形]]」[[歌川国芳]] 安政2年 File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|[[根付け]]Hideaki (or Shūmei) 19th File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|根付け Matsushige 18th File:Unryôsai Hiroyasu - Tsuba with Legendary Figures by the Seashore - Walters 51147.jpg|[[鍔]] Unryôsai Hiroyasu 19th </gallery>
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Ashinagatenaga}} * [[巨人]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:てなかあしなか}} [[Category:巨人]] [[Category:日本の民間信仰]]

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|ja|河鍋暁斎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanabe Kyōsai|qq=}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[Akita Prefecture]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], [[Fukushima Prefecture]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], and [[Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|qq=}}, dispatched a three-legged [[crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|ja|円仁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ennin|qq=}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|ja|鳥海山大物忌神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|qq=}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|qq=}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Province|qq=}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}), the {{ill|Amanotanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanotanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|qq=}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|ja|群書類従|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa|ja|諏訪市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa|qq=}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|qq=}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayama Festival|qq=}} of {{ill|Hida|ja|飛騨|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hida|qq=}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|ja|歌川国芳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utagawa Kuniyoshi|qq=}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
