While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, dispatched a three-legged [[:en:Crow|Crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now [[:en:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''[[:en:Gunsho Ruijū|Gunsho Ruijū]]'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]], Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the [[:en:Takayama Festival|Takayama Festival]] of [[:en:Hida|Hida]].<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by [[:en:Utagawa Kuniyoshi|Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now [[:en:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]), leading to their disappearance. In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''[[:en:Gunsho Ruijū|Gunsho Ruijū]]'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]], Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the [[:en:Takayama Festival|Takayama Festival]] of [[:en:Hida|Hida]].<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by [[:en:Utagawa Kuniyoshi|Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

[[ja:手長足長]]
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:手長足長]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11497171}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11497171]] Wikidata name: Tenaga-Ashinaga [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[:en:Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by [[:en:Kawanabe Kyōsai|Kawanabe Kyōsai]]]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, dispatched a three-legged [[:en:Crow|Crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now [[:en:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>

Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.

In [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]], Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the [[:en:Takayama Festival|Takayama Festival]] of [[:en:Hida|Hida]].<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by [[:en:Utagawa Kuniyoshi|Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''[[:en:Gunsho Ruijū|Gunsho Ruijū]]'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]], Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the [[:en:Takayama Festival|Takayama Festival]] of [[:en:Hida|Hida]].<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by [[:en:Utagawa Kuniyoshi|Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

[[ja:手長足長]]
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:手長足長]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11497171}} [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11497171]] Wikidata name: Tenaga-Ashinaga [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[:en:Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by [[:en:Kawanabe Kyōsai|Kawanabe Kyōsai]]]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, dispatched a three-legged [[:en:Crow|Crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now [[:en:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''[[:en:Gunsho Ruijū|Gunsho Ruijū]]'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]], Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the [[:en:Takayama Festival|Takayama Festival]] of [[:en:Hida|Hida]].<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by [[:en:Utagawa Kuniyoshi|Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

In [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]], Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the [[:en:Takayama Festival|Takayama Festival]] of [[:en:Hida|Hida]].<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by [[:en:Utagawa Kuniyoshi|Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Pages with conflicting qids]] to [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11497171]] Wikidata name: Tenaga-Ashinaga [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[:en:Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|[[:en:Yōkai|Yōkai]] Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by [[:en:Kawanabe Kyōsai|Kawanabe Kyōsai]]]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]], [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]] in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on [[:en:Mount Chōkai|Mount Chōkai]], possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the [[:en:Sea of Japan|Sea of Japan]]. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, dispatched a three-legged [[:en:Crow|Crow]] to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until [[:en:Ennin|Ennin]] (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now [[:en:Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine]]), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered [[:en:Machilus_thunbergii|Tabunoki]] seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for [[:en:Mount_Bandai|Mount Bandai]]. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''[[:en:Gunsho Ruijū|Gunsho Ruijū]]'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In [[:en:Suwa, Nagano|Suwa]], Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as [[:en:Sancai_Tuhui|Sancai Tuhui]]. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the [[:en:Takayama Festival|Takayama Festival]] of [[:en:Hida|Hida]].<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by [[:en:Utagawa Kuniyoshi|Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|en|Gunsho Ruijū|WD=Q1554852|lt=Gunsho Ruijū}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|WD=Q846338|lt=Suwa}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|Sancai_Tuhui|en|Sancai_Tuhui|WD=Q1761479|lt=Sancai Tuhui}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|en|Takayama Festival|WD=Q7677467|lt=Takayama Festival}} of {{ill|Hida|en|Hida|WD=Q363858|lt=Hida}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|en|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|WD=Q317736|lt=Utagawa Kuniyoshi}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Giants (mythology)|Giants (mythology)]]

{{Omonoimi Faith}}

{{wikidata link|Q11497171}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Giants]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese folklore]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11497171]] Wikidata name: Tenaga-Ashinaga [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[:en:Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|{{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|WD=Q503186|lt=Yōkai}} Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|en|Kawanabe Kyōsai|WD=Q2838030|lt=Kawanabe Kyōsai}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}} in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chōkai}}, possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}}. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, dispatched a three-legged {{ill|Crow|en|Crow|WD=Q388450|lt=Crow}} to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|WD=Q732282|lt=Ennin}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|en|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|WD=Q11173679|lt=Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered {{ill|Machilus_thunbergii|en|Machilus_thunbergii|WD=Q5792073|lt=Tabunoki}} seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for {{ill|Mount_Bandai|en|Mount_Bandai|WD=Q714605|lt=Mount Bandai}}. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|en|Echizen Province|WD=Q1047144|lt=Echizen Province}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|en|Gunsho Ruijū|WD=Q1554852|lt=Gunsho Ruijū}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|WD=Q846338|lt=Suwa}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|Sancai_Tuhui|en|Sancai_Tuhui|WD=Q1761479|lt=Sancai Tuhui}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|en|Takayama Festival|WD=Q7677467|lt=Takayama Festival}} of {{ill|Hida|en|Hida|WD=Q363858|lt=Hida}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|en|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|WD=Q317736|lt=Utagawa Kuniyoshi}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|WD=Q732282|lt=Ennin}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|en|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|WD=Q11173679|lt=Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered {{ill|Machilus_thunbergii|en|Machilus_thunbergii|WD=Q5792073|lt=Tabunoki}} seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for {{ill|Mount_Bandai|en|Mount_Bandai|WD=Q714605|lt=Mount Bandai}}. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|en|Echizen Province|WD=Q1047144|lt=Echizen Province}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|en|Gunsho Ruijū|WD=Q1554852|lt=Gunsho Ruijū}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|WD=Q846338|lt=Suwa}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|Sancai_Tuhui|en|Sancai_Tuhui|WD=Q1761479|lt=Sancai Tuhui}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|en|Takayama Festival|WD=Q7677467|lt=Takayama Festival}} of {{ill|Hida|en|Hida|WD=Q363858|lt=Hida}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|en|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|WD=Q317736|lt=Utagawa Kuniyoshi}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:手長足長]][[zh:手長足長]]
{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[:en:Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|{{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|WD=Q503186|lt=Yōkai}} Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|en|Kawanabe Kyōsai|WD=Q2838030|lt=Kawanabe Kyōsai}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}} in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chōkai}}, possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan}}. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, dispatched a three-legged {{ill|Crow|en|Crow|WD=Q388450|lt=Crow}} to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|WD=Q732282|lt=Ennin}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|en|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|WD=Q11173679|lt=Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered {{ill|Machilus_thunbergii|en|Machilus_thunbergii|WD=Q5792073|lt=Tabunoki}} seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for {{ill|Mount_Bandai|en|Mount_Bandai|WD=Q714605|lt=Mount Bandai}}. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|en|Echizen Province|WD=Q1047144|lt=Echizen Province}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|en|Gunsho Ruijū|WD=Q1554852|lt=Gunsho Ruijū}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|WD=Q846338|lt=Suwa}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|Sancai_Tuhui|en|Sancai_Tuhui|WD=Q1761479|lt=Sancai Tuhui}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>

The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|en|Takayama Festival|WD=Q7677467|lt=Takayama Festival}} of {{ill|Hida|en|Hida|WD=Q363858|lt=Hida}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|en|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|WD=Q317736|lt=Utagawa Kuniyoshi}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>

{{Otheruses|Japanese legends of giants such as [[:en:Giants (mythology)|giants]] named Tenaga and Ashinaga|{{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|WD=Q503186|lt=Yōkai|1=Yōkai}} Ashinaga Tenaga|Ashinaga Tenaga}} [[File:Kyosai Tenaga-Ashinaga-zu.jpg|right|thumb|120px|''Tenaga Ashinaga'' by {{ill|Kawanabe Kyōsai|en|Kawanabe Kyōsai|WD=Q2838030|lt=Kawanabe Kyōsai|1=Kawanabe Kyōsai}}]] '''Tenaga and Ashinaga''' (手長足長) are legendary giants appearing in myths and folktales from regions such as {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture|1=Fukui Prefecture}} in Japan.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Kenji Murakami (ed.)|title=Yōkai Jiten|year=2000|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|isbn=978-4-620-31428-0|pages=228-229}}</ref>
== Overview ==
Their defining trait is their abnormally long limbs. While accounts across regions describe this shared characteristic, variations exist: Tenaga and Ashinaga may be depicted as a single giant with long arms and legs, as a married couple with the husband having long legs and the wife having long arms, or as sibling giants. "Tenaga" refers to the one with long arms, while "Ashinaga" describes the one with long legs.
== Local Legends and Folktales ==
=== Akita and Yamagata ===
In Akita, it is said that Tenaga and Ashinaga resided on {{ill|Mount Chōkai|en|Mount Chōkai|WD=Q713826|lt=Mount Chōkai|1=Mount Chōkai}}, possessing limbs so long they could span mountains. They reportedly kidnapped travelers and attacked ships on the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|WD=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}}. The god of Mount Chōkai, {{ill|Ōmonoimi no Kami|ja|大物忌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmonoimi no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, dispatched a three-legged {{ill|Crow|en|Crow|WD=Q388450|lt=Crow|1=Crow}} to warn people, crying "Aruya" when they appeared and "Muya" when they were absent. The term "Aruya-Muya Barrier" (有耶無耶の関) at the base of the mountain is derived from this legend.<ref name="murakami" /><ref name="yuza">{{Cite web|url=http://www.town.yuza.yamagata.jp/Files/1/6007/html/%E4%B8%89%E5%B4%8E%E5%85%AC%E5%9C%92.html|title=Misaki Park and the Aruya-Muya Barrier|publisher=[[Yuza Town]] Industry Promotion Division|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
Despite these measures, their misdeeds continued until {{ill|Ennin|en|Ennin|WD=Q732282|lt=Ennin|1=Ennin}} (Jikaku Daishi) prayed for 100 days at Fukura Shrine (now {{ill|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|en|Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|WD=Q11173679|lt=Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine|1=Chōkaisan Ōmonoimi Shrine}}), leading to their disappearance. Some accounts say they surrendered to the monk, ceasing their misdeeds. Before leaving, he scattered {{ill|Machilus_thunbergii|en|Machilus_thunbergii|WD=Q5792073|lt=Tabunoki|1=Machilus thunbergii}} seeds for them to eat, and these trees still flourish in the Misaki area.<ref name="yuza" />
=== Fukushima ===
In Fukushima, the giants were said to inhabit Mount Byōnōzan, an ancient name for {{ill|Mount_Bandai|en|Mount_Bandai|WD=Q714605|lt=Mount Bandai|1=Mount Bandai}}. They covered the land with clouds, rendering the region barren. A traveling monk, learning of their deeds, sealed them at the mountain’s peak, establishing the shrine {{ill|Bandai Myōjin|ja|磐梯明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bandai Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. This act led to the renaming of the mountain as Mount Bandai.<ref>[http://www.kennan-syuhan.co.jp/furusato/036.html "Furusato Fukushima Journey Episode 36: Mount Bandai and Kōbō Daishi"] - Fukushima Prefecture Southern Liquor Corporation</ref>
=== Fukui ===
At {{ill|Ōshima|ja|雄島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshima|qq=}}, the giants were the original inhabitants of {{ill|Echizen Province|en|Echizen Province|WD=Q1047144|lt=Echizen Province|1=Echizen Province}}. Ashinaga would carry Tenaga on his back into the sea, where Tenaga used his long arms to catch fish.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.net24.ne.jp/~oominato/01/r/03.html|title=The Inhabitants of Ajima: Tenaga and Ashinaga|accessdate=2014-05-22}}</ref>
== Deification of Tenaga and Ashinaga ==
Separate from their fearsome image, Tenaga and Ashinaga also appear as divine or supernatural beings.
According to the ''{{ill|Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|ja|大日本国一宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki|qq=}}'' (compiled in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}), the {{ill|Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|ja|天手長男神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Tanagao Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Iki|ja|壱岐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 壱岐島|1=Iki Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q494914}} was considered the region's primary shrine.<ref>''Dai Nippon Koku Ichinomiya Ki'' (in ''{{ill|Gunsho Ruijū|en|Gunsho Ruijū|WD=Q1554852|lt=Gunsho Ruijū|1=Gunsho Ruijū}}'', Vol. 2, Jingi Section, 1932), p. 246</ref>
In {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|WD=Q846338|lt=Suwa|1=Suwa, Nagano}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|Sancai_Tuhui|en|Sancai_Tuhui|WD=Q1761479|lt=Sancai Tuhui|1=Sancai Tuhui}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|en|Takayama Festival|WD=Q7677467|lt=Takayama Festival|1=Takayama Festival}} of {{ill|Hida|en|Hida|WD=Q363858|lt=Hida|1=Hida}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|en|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|WD=Q317736|lt=Utagawa Kuniyoshi|1=Utagawa Kuniyoshi}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

In {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|WD=Q846338|lt=Suwa|1=Suwa, Nagano}}, Nagano, Tenaga and Ashinaga were regarded as retainers of the {{ill|Suwa Myōjin|ja|諏訪明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諏訪神社|1=Suwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2370082}} and worshiped at the {{ill|Tenaga Shrine|ja|手長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenaga Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Ashinaga Shrine|ja|足長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashinaga Shrine|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080403203449/http://www.geocities.jp/sizen_junnosuke/ichigojinjya17.html|title=Strawberry Shrine Visit Tales: Tenaga Shrine and Ashinaga Shrine|accessdate=2009-01-31}}</ref>
== Influence from China ==
Legends of long-armed and long-legged beings appear in Chinese works such as {{ill|Sancai_Tuhui|en|Sancai_Tuhui|WD=Q1761479|lt=Sancai Tuhui|1=Sancai Tuhui}}. These beings were adapted into Japanese art to symbolize wishes for the emperor's longevity.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Kōji Hosaka|title=Dai Kagami: Full Modern Translation|publisher=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1998|isbn=4-06-158491-X}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions ==
The motif of Tenaga and Ashinaga can be found in art and festivals, such as carvings on bridges and floats in the {{ill|Takayama Festival|en|Takayama Festival|WD=Q7677467|lt=Takayama Festival|1=Takayama Festival}} of {{ill|Hida|en|Hida|WD=Q363858|lt=Hida|1=Hida}}.<ref name="murakami" />
<gallery> File:NDL-DC 1310282-Utagawa Kuniyoshi-浅草奥山生人形-安政2-cmb.jpg|''Asakusa Okuyama Dolls,'' by {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|en|Utagawa Kuniyoshi|WD=Q317736|lt=Utagawa Kuniyoshi|1=Utagawa Kuniyoshi}} File:Ashinaga and Tenaga LACMA AC1998.249.194.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Hideaki, 19th century File:Matsushige - Ashinaga ("Long-legs") and Tenaga ("Long-arms") Catching a Fish - Walters 71844.jpg|''Netsuke'' by Matsushige, 18th century </gallery>
